# LinkedIn Posts & Blog — Reflect Feature Launch

> **Series strategy:** 2 LinkedIn posts + 1 blog post, building a narrative arc toward v4.0 release.
> **Tone:** Thinking out loud, inviting engagement. NOT a product announcement until the blog.

---

## Content Plan

| Content | When | Purpose |
|---------|------|---------|
| **Post 1** (published) | Mar 8 | Discovery — Claude Code's `/insights` reveals a gap |
| **Post 2** (LinkedIn) | Mar 11 | "Thinking out loud" — the hard problems I'm hitting while building cross-session AI analysis. Invite engagement. |
| *[engagement window]* | Mar 11-13 | Reply to comments, ask follow-ups, let the conversation develop |
| **Post 3** (LinkedIn) | Mar 14-15 | Learnings + what we built + tease the release. Bridge to the blog. |
| **v4.0 release** | ~Mar 19-20 | Ship when solid |
| **Blog post** | Same day as v4.0 | The canonical deep-dive. Comprehensive journey + technical decisions. |

---

## Post 1: The Discovery Story (PUBLISHED)

**Title:** Claude Code showed me my own patterns — and I couldn't unsee the gap

*(Already published — see previous version for full text)*

---

## Post 2: Design Principles for LLM-Powered Analysis (Conversation Starter)

**Title idea:** Building an LLM feature that analyzes human-AI collaboration taught me three design principles I haven't seen written down anywhere.

**Goal:** Share high-level design principles with concrete examples from the build. Position as someone extracting transferable ideas, not stumbling through problems. Invite engagement on the principles themselves.

---

In my last post, I shared how Claude Code's `/insights` command analyzed 128 of my AI coding sessions and surfaced patterns I couldn't see. It inspired me to build the same capability into Code Insights — but working across 5 AI tools, not just one.

The build has surfaced some design principles that I think apply broadly to anyone building LLM-powered analysis features. Here are three.

**1. Separate the computation boundary from the synthesis boundary.**

When you're building a feature that analyzes data across many records — sessions, documents, conversations, whatever — there's a temptation to hand the LLM the raw data and ask it to "find patterns." This collapses two fundamentally different operations into one prompt.

Computation (counting, ranking, grouping, averaging) is deterministic. Synthesis (interpreting trends, explaining causes, generating narratives) is generative. These need different execution environments.

In practice: we aggregate all session facets in SQL — frequency counts, severity distributions, co-occurrence patterns — and pass the LLM pre-computed statistics. The prompt explicitly states that every claim must trace back to provided data. The LLM's job is to interpret the numbers, not produce them.

This isn't about LLM limitations. It's an architectural principle. The same way you wouldn't embed business logic in your view layer, you shouldn't embed data aggregation in your generative layer. The boundaries exist for testability, debuggability, and trust. When a synthesis claim looks wrong, I can check whether the underlying numbers are wrong (SQL bug) or the interpretation is wrong (prompt issue). Without that boundary, every error is opaque.

**2. Classification taxonomies for LLMs need contrastive definitions, not just descriptions.**

When designing categories that an LLM will classify into, the natural instinct is to describe what each category means. But descriptions create overlap — and overlapping categories produce inconsistent classification.

The breakthrough was contrastive guidance: defining each category by what it ISN'T, relative to its most confusable neighbor.

Example from our friction taxonomy: "wrong-approach" and "knowledge-gap" look similar on the surface. Both involve the AI doing something incorrect. The contrastive definition: *"If the AI had access to the right information but selected the wrong method, that's wrong-approach. If the AI lacked critical information that would have changed the approach entirely, that's knowledge-gap."* This draws a decision boundary, not just a description.

We apply the same principle to effective patterns. "Context-gathering" and "domain-expertise" are easy to conflate. The contrastive rule: *"If the AI actively investigated the codebase first (reading files, searching), it's context-gathering. If it applied existing knowledge without investigation, it's domain-expertise."* Observable behavior becomes the discriminator.

This mirrors how human experts classify ambiguous cases — not by matching to a definition, but by distinguishing between the two most likely categories. Designing for the confusion boundary rather than the center of each category is the key insight.

**3. In human-AI collaboration analysis, separate the WHAT from the WHO.**

This is the design decision I'm most interested in hearing other perspectives on.

When analyzing a coding session where a human and an AI worked together, every friction point and every effective technique has two orthogonal dimensions: what happened (the category) and who contributed to it (the attribution).

"Wrong approach" in a session could stem from unclear user instructions, an AI capability limitation, or outdated documentation. Same category — completely different causal chain. If you bake the actor into the category ("AI-hallucination" vs "user-miscommunication"), your taxonomy doubles in size and every category becomes a blend of symptom and cause.

We separated these into independent fields. The category describes the phenomenon in actor-neutral language. A separate attribution field classifies it as user-actionable, ai-capability, or environmental. For effective patterns, a parallel driver field captures whether the technique was user-driven, ai-driven, or collaborative.

The practical payoff: you can now ask "am I getting better at working with AI?" by filtering on user-actionable friction over time — isolating your growth signal from improvements in the AI itself. Without this separation, progress tracking measures a blend that's impossible to decompose.

But there's a subtlety worth noting. When you ask an LLM to assign attribution for AI-assisted work, the model has a natural self-critical bias — it tends to attribute more friction to itself than the evidence supports. Actor-neutral category descriptions and a structured decision tree (external factor? → user-preventable? → AI capability) help counteract this, but it's an area where I expect the calibration to evolve as we get more data.

**I'd genuinely like to hear from others building features where LLMs analyze or evaluate collaborative work.** How do you think about separating the dimensions of classification? Has anyone found approaches to the attribution problem that go beyond what I've described here?

---

## Post 3: What We Built + Learnings (Bridge to Release)

**Title idea:** I asked AI to analyze 40 of my AI coding sessions. Here's what it found — and what I learned building it.

**Goal:** Share the payoff. What Reflect actually surfaced. The closing-the-loop story. Tease v4 and point to the blog.

---

A few days ago I shared the hard problems I hit building cross-session AI analysis for Code Insights. The response was incredible — turns out a lot of people building with LLMs are wrestling with the same classification and attribution challenges.

Here's what happened next.

**The taxonomy landed at 9 friction categories and 8 effective pattern categories.**

After iterating through free-form output, fuzzy normalization, and contrastive classification guidance, the taxonomy stabilized. The friction categories are tuned for AI coding sessions specifically — not generic software categories:

wrong-approach, knowledge-gap, stale-assumptions, incomplete-requirements, context-loss, scope-creep, repeated-mistakes, documentation-gap, tooling-limitation

Each one carries an attribution: was this user-actionable, an AI capability limitation, or environmental?

**The effective patterns tell you what's working — and who's driving it.**

Same idea, opposite direction. 8 pattern categories (structured-planning, incremental-implementation, verification-workflow, systematic-debugging, self-correction, context-gathering, domain-expertise, effective-tooling) each with a driver field: user-driven, ai-driven, or collaborative.

This matters because "the AI is great at X" is different from "I'm great at X with AI." Progress tracking needs to know the difference.

**I added prompt quality analysis with 5 dimension scores.**

Every session gets scored on: context provision, request specificity, scope management, information timing, and correction quality. Each on a 0-100 scale. Plus specific before/after rewrite suggestions.

This was the "eat your own cooking" moment. Seeing my own prompt quality scores was... humbling. My context provision was strong, but my scope management needed work — I tend to expand scope mid-session.

**Then I ran it on my own 40 sessions. And actually used the output.**

Reflect surfaced 30 friction occurrences across my sessions. The top patterns: "wrong-approach" (12x), "agent-orchestration-failure" (11x), "api-misunderstanding" (7x).

But the real value was the artifacts it generated. Targeted CLAUDE.md rules and hook configs based on MY specific friction patterns. I integrated them the same day:

- 3 new CLAUDE.md sections — Pre-Action Verification, Retry Discipline, Unresponsive Agent Protocol — covering all 30 top friction occurrences
- A hookify rule that fires a warning before any git checkout to an existing branch
- Updated agent prompts — my engineer agent no longer hardcodes main, my PM agent escalates unresponsive sub-agents instead of waiting

The insights went from "interesting report" to "enforced workflow rules" in the same session. That's the difference between reading about your patterns and actually fixing them.

**The LLM engineering lesson I keep coming back to:**

Save the LLM's chain-of-thought reasoning to your database. We added a `_reasoning` field that the model fills before classifying — it's never shown to users, but it creates a prompt-tuning dataset for free. When a friction point is miscategorized, I can see exactly what the model was thinking and adjust the prompt. Cost: ~190 extra tokens per call. Value: eliminates guesswork when debugging classification quality.

This feature is called "Reflect" and it's coming to Code Insights as part of v4.0. It works across all 5 supported AI tools — Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI, Copilot CLI, and VS Code Copilot Chat. Fully local, no cloud, no accounts.

Release and a full technical deep-dive blog post coming soon.

---

## Blog Post: The Full Journey (Ships with v4.0)

**Title:** Building Cross-Session AI Analysis: From Claude Code's /insights to Code Insights v4.0

**Format:** 1,500-2,500 word narrative technical post (Linear/Notion engineering blog style)

**Outline:**

1. **The Catalyst** — Running `/insights`, what it showed, the gap analysis
2. **The 4-Day Build** — Architecture decisions, the full stack sprint
3. **The Prototype Trap** — Why the initial 15-category taxonomy failed
4. **Taxonomy Evolution** — Free-form → normalized → curated. The three-phase journey.
5. **The Attribution Problem** — Separating WHAT from WHO. Actor-neutral descriptions. The 3-step decision tree.
6. **Prompt Engineering for Classification** — Contrastive guidance, baseline exclusion, CoT-to-DB
7. **Prompt Quality Analysis** — 5 dimension scores, two-layer output (takeaways vs findings)
8. **Weekly Reflections** — ISO week navigation, making it a habit not a one-time report
9. **Closing the Loop** — Running Reflect on my own sessions, integrating the output
10. **What's Next** — Progress tracking, friction-to-pattern affinity maps

**Publish at:** code-insights.app/blog or Medium

---

## Post 2 — Variant B: Contrastive Classification (Journey Version) ⭐ PREFERRED

**Title idea:** It took 3 iterations to get LLM classification right. Here's the technique that worked.

---

I'm building a feature that analyzes AI coding sessions across 5 tools and classifies friction points into categories. Getting that classification consistent took three iterations.

**Attempt 1: Let the LLM decide.**

Free-form output. "Classify the friction in this session." Across 40 sessions, the same concept appeared as: "api-misunderstanding", "API-Misunderstanding", "api-confusion", "misunderstood-api", "api-knowledge-gap." Agent coordination issues split into: "agent-orchestration-failure", "agent-communication-failure", "agent-communication-breakdown", "agent-lifecycle-issue", "agent-shutdown-failure."

15 intended categories became 40+ variants. Cross-session aggregation was meaningless.

**Attempt 2: Define the categories + normalize the output.**

I curated 15 canonical categories and built a normalization layer — fuzzy string matching, alias maps that remap variants like "missing-dependency", "config-drift", "stale-cache", "version-mismatch" all to a single "stale-assumptions" category.

Better — but still inconsistent at the boundaries. "wrong-approach" and "knowledge-gap" got swapped constantly. Both involve the AI doing something incorrect. The descriptions overlapped. Same with "context-loss" vs "stale-assumptions" — both involve working from outdated information.

15 categories was also too many. Several were symptoms of the same root cause.

**Attempt 3: Fewer categories. Contrastive definitions. Attribution as a separate dimension.**

Consolidated to 9 categories: wrong-approach, knowledge-gap, stale-assumptions, incomplete-requirements, context-loss, scope-creep, repeated-mistakes, documentation-gap, tooling-limitation.

But the real shift was defining each category by what it ISN'T — relative to its most confusable neighbor:

*"If the AI had the right information but selected the wrong method → wrong-approach. If the AI lacked critical information that would have changed the approach entirely → knowledge-gap."*

*"If prior decisions from earlier in the session were forgotten → context-loss. If work started from assumptions about external state that turned out to be wrong → stale-assumptions."*

This is contrastive guidance — decision boundaries between confusable pairs, not descriptions of category centers. It mirrors how human experts handle ambiguous cases: by ruling out the closest alternative.

And instead of baking WHO caused the friction into the category name, we separated it into an independent attribution field: user-actionable, ai-capability, or environmental. Same "wrong-approach" category, but now you can filter by whether better user input would have prevented it or whether the AI failed despite clear instructions.

Classification consistency jumped. Not because we found better categories — because we changed the decision logic.

**These 9 categories are what we're shipping.** I'd genuinely like to hear: do they match the friction patterns you see in your AI coding sessions? What's missing? What would you rename?

wrong-approach · knowledge-gap · stale-assumptions · incomplete-requirements · context-loss · scope-creep · repeated-mistakes · documentation-gap · tooling-limitation

---

## Post 2 — Variant C: Condensed (All Three Principles)

**Title idea:** Three design principles from building LLM-powered session analysis

---

I'm building a feature that analyzes AI coding sessions across 5 tools — Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI, Copilot CLI, VS Code Copilot Chat — to surface friction patterns and effective techniques. Three design principles emerged that I think apply broadly.

**1. Separate computation from synthesis.** Aggregate in SQL. Synthesize with the LLM. Never ask the model to count, rank, or compute percentages — pass it pre-computed statistics and let it interpret. Same reason you don't embed business logic in the view layer: testability, debuggability, trust.

**2. Design taxonomies with contrastive definitions.** Describing what each category means creates overlap. Defining what each category ISN'T — relative to its most confusable neighbor — draws decision boundaries. "If the AI had the right info but chose wrong → wrong-approach. If it lacked critical info → knowledge-gap." Classification consistency improved significantly.

**3. Separate the WHAT from the WHO.** Every friction point has two orthogonal dimensions: what happened (category) and who contributed (attribution: user-actionable, ai-capability, or environmental). Baking the actor into the category doubles your taxonomy and makes progress tracking impossible — you can't isolate "am I getting better?" from "is the AI getting better?"

These compound. Deterministic aggregation feeds contrastive classification feeds orthogonal attribution. Each principle makes the next one work.

Building this in the open as part of Code Insights. **Would love to hear from others — what design patterns have you found essential when building LLM-powered analysis features?**

---

## Posting Notes

### Post 2 (This week — conversation starter)
- **Angle:** "Thinking out loud" about 3 hard problems. Invites engagement. Does NOT announce anything.
- **Visuals:** Optional — could do a simple before/after of messy vs clean categories, or skip visuals entirely (text-only posts often perform well for thought pieces)
- **CTA:** Open question — "How do you handle output consistency?" + "Has anyone tackled attribution?"
- **Engagement plan:** Reply to every comment within first 2 hours. Ask follow-up questions. Let the conversation run 1-2 days.

### Post 3 (2-3 days after Post 2)
- **Angle:** The payoff. What Reflect found. The closing-the-loop story. Teases v4 + blog.
- **Visuals:** Dashboard screenshot showing friction categories + attribution, or the Patterns page hero card
- **CTA:** Dual — "Full deep-dive coming with the release" + GitHub link for the curious

### Blog Post (Release day)
- **Angle:** Comprehensive technical narrative. The canonical reference.
- **Visuals:** Architecture diagram, before/after screenshots, taxonomy table

# LinkedIn Posts & Blog — Reflect Feature Launch

> **Series strategy:** 2 LinkedIn posts + 1 blog post, building a narrative arc toward v4.0 release.
> **Tone:** Thinking out loud, inviting engagement. NOT a product announcement until the blog.

---

## Content Plan

| Content | When | Purpose |
|---------|------|---------|
| **Post 1** (published) | Mar 8 | Discovery — Claude Code's `/insights` reveals a gap |
| **Post 2** (LinkedIn) | When ready | "Thinking out loud" — the hard problems I'm hitting while building cross-session AI analysis. Invite engagement. |
| *[engagement window]* | 2-3 days after Post 2 | Reply to comments, ask follow-ups, let the conversation develop |
| **Post 3** (LinkedIn) | 2-3 days after engagement | Learnings + what we built + tease the release. Bridge to the blog. |
| **v4.0 release** | When solid | Ship alongside blog |
| **Blog post** | Same day as v4.0 | The canonical deep-dive. Comprehensive journey + technical decisions. |

---

## Post 1: The Discovery Story (PUBLISHED)

**Title:** Claude Code showed me my own patterns — and I couldn't unsee the gap

*(Already published — see previous version for full text)*

---

## Post 2: Design Principles for LLM-Powered Analysis (Conversation Starter)

**Title idea:** Building an LLM feature that analyzes human-AI collaboration taught me three design principles I haven't seen written down anywhere.

**Goal:** Share high-level design principles with concrete examples from the build. Position as someone extracting transferable ideas, not stumbling through problems. Invite engagement on the principles themselves.

---

In my last post, I shared how Claude Code's `/insights` command analyzed 128 of my AI coding sessions and surfaced patterns I couldn't see. It inspired me to build the same capability into Code Insights — but working across 5 AI tools, not just one.

The build has surfaced some design principles that I think apply broadly to anyone building LLM-powered analysis features. Here are three.

**1. Separate the computation boundary from the synthesis boundary.**

When you're building a feature that analyzes data across many records — sessions, documents, conversations, whatever — there's a temptation to hand the LLM the raw data and ask it to "find patterns." This collapses two fundamentally different operations into one prompt.

Computation (counting, ranking, grouping, averaging) is deterministic. Synthesis (interpreting trends, explaining causes, generating narratives) is generative. These need different execution environments.

In practice: we aggregate all session facets in SQL — frequency counts, severity distributions, co-occurrence patterns — and pass the LLM pre-computed statistics. The prompt explicitly states that every claim must trace back to provided data. The LLM's job is to interpret the numbers, not produce them.

This isn't about LLM limitations. It's an architectural principle. The same way you wouldn't embed business logic in your view layer, you shouldn't embed data aggregation in your generative layer. The boundaries exist for testability, debuggability, and trust. When a synthesis claim looks wrong, I can check whether the underlying numbers are wrong (SQL bug) or the interpretation is wrong (prompt issue). Without that boundary, every error is opaque.

**2. Classification taxonomies for LLMs need contrastive definitions, not just descriptions.**

When designing categories that an LLM will classify into, the natural instinct is to describe what each category means. But descriptions create overlap — and overlapping categories produce inconsistent classification.

The breakthrough was contrastive guidance: defining each category by what it ISN'T, relative to its most confusable neighbor.

Example from our friction taxonomy: "wrong-approach" and "knowledge-gap" look similar on the surface. Both involve the AI doing something incorrect. The contrastive definition: *"If the AI had access to the right information but selected the wrong method, that's wrong-approach. If the AI lacked critical information that would have changed the approach entirely, that's knowledge-gap."* This draws a decision boundary, not just a description.

We apply the same principle to effective patterns. "Context-gathering" and "domain-expertise" are easy to conflate. The contrastive rule: *"If the AI actively investigated the codebase first (reading files, searching), it's context-gathering. If it applied existing knowledge without investigation, it's domain-expertise."* Observable behavior becomes the discriminator.

This mirrors how human experts classify ambiguous cases — not by matching to a definition, but by distinguishing between the two most likely categories. Designing for the confusion boundary rather than the center of each category is the key insight.

**3. In human-AI collaboration analysis, separate the WHAT from the WHO.**

This is the design decision I'm most interested in hearing other perspectives on.

When analyzing a coding session where a human and an AI worked together, every friction point and every effective technique has two orthogonal dimensions: what happened (the category) and who contributed to it (the attribution).

"Wrong approach" in a session could stem from unclear user instructions, an AI capability limitation, or outdated documentation. Same category — completely different causal chain. If you bake the actor into the category ("AI-hallucination" vs "user-miscommunication"), your taxonomy doubles in size and every category becomes a blend of symptom and cause.

We separated these into independent fields. The category describes the phenomenon in actor-neutral language. A separate attribution field classifies it as user-actionable, ai-capability, or environmental. For effective patterns, a parallel driver field captures whether the technique was user-driven, ai-driven, or collaborative.

The practical payoff: you can now ask "am I getting better at working with AI?" by filtering on user-actionable friction over time — isolating your growth signal from improvements in the AI itself. Without this separation, progress tracking measures a blend that's impossible to decompose.

But there's a subtlety worth noting. When you ask an LLM to assign attribution for AI-assisted work, the model has a natural self-critical bias — it tends to attribute more friction to itself than the evidence supports. Actor-neutral category descriptions and a structured decision tree (external factor? → user-preventable? → AI capability) help counteract this, but it's an area where I expect the calibration to evolve as we get more data.

**I'd genuinely like to hear from others building features where LLMs analyze or evaluate collaborative work.** How do you think about separating the dimensions of classification? Has anyone found approaches to the attribution problem that go beyond what I've described here?

---

## Post 3: What We Built + Learnings (Bridge to Release)

**Title idea:** I asked AI to analyze 40 of my AI coding sessions. Here's what it found — and what I learned building it.

**Goal:** Share the payoff. What Reflect actually surfaced. The closing-the-loop story. Tease v4 and point to the blog.

---

A few days ago I shared the hard problems I hit building cross-session AI analysis for Code Insights. The response was incredible — turns out a lot of people building with LLMs are wrestling with the same classification and attribution challenges.

Here's what happened next.

**The taxonomy landed at 9 friction categories and 8 effective pattern categories.**

After iterating through free-form output, fuzzy normalization, and contrastive classification guidance, the taxonomy stabilized. The friction categories are tuned for AI coding sessions specifically — not generic software categories:

wrong-approach, knowledge-gap, stale-assumptions, incomplete-requirements, context-loss, scope-creep, repeated-mistakes, documentation-gap, tooling-limitation

Each one carries an attribution: was this user-actionable, an AI capability limitation, or environmental?

**The effective patterns tell you what's working — and who's driving it.**

Same idea, opposite direction. 8 pattern categories (structured-planning, incremental-implementation, verification-workflow, systematic-debugging, self-correction, context-gathering, domain-expertise, effective-tooling) each with a driver field: user-driven, ai-driven, or collaborative.

This matters because "the AI is great at X" is different from "I'm great at X with AI." Progress tracking needs to know the difference.

**I added prompt quality analysis with 5 dimension scores.**

Every session gets scored on: context provision, request specificity, scope management, information timing, and correction quality. Each on a 0-100 scale. Plus specific before/after rewrite suggestions.

This was the "eat your own cooking" moment. Seeing my own prompt quality scores was... humbling. My context provision was strong, but my scope management needed work — I tend to expand scope mid-session.

**Then I ran it on my own 40 sessions. And actually used the output.**

Reflect surfaced 30 friction occurrences across my sessions. The top patterns: "wrong-approach" (12x), "agent-orchestration-failure" (11x), "api-misunderstanding" (7x).

But the real value was the artifacts it generated. Targeted CLAUDE.md rules and hook configs based on MY specific friction patterns. I integrated them the same day:

- 3 new CLAUDE.md sections — Pre-Action Verification, Retry Discipline, Unresponsive Agent Protocol — covering all 30 top friction occurrences
- A hookify rule that fires a warning before any git checkout to an existing branch
- Updated agent prompts — my engineer agent no longer hardcodes main, my PM agent escalates unresponsive sub-agents instead of waiting

The insights went from "interesting report" to "enforced workflow rules" in the same session. That's the difference between reading about your patterns and actually fixing them.

**The LLM engineering lesson I keep coming back to:**

Save the LLM's chain-of-thought reasoning to your database. We added a `_reasoning` field that the model fills before classifying — it's never shown to users, but it creates a prompt-tuning dataset for free. When a friction point is miscategorized, I can see exactly what the model was thinking and adjust the prompt. Cost: ~190 extra tokens per call. Value: eliminates guesswork when debugging classification quality.

This feature is called "Reflect" and it's coming to Code Insights as part of v4.0. It works across all 5 supported AI tools — Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI, Copilot CLI, and VS Code Copilot Chat. Fully local, no cloud, no accounts.

Release and a full technical deep-dive blog post coming soon.

---

## Blog Post: The Full Journey (Ships with v4.0)

**Title:** Building Cross-Session AI Analysis: From Claude Code's /insights to Code Insights v4.0

**Format:** 1,500-2,500 word narrative technical post (Linear/Notion engineering blog style)

**Outline:**

1. **The Catalyst** — Running `/insights`, what it showed, the gap analysis
2. **The 4-Day Build** — Architecture decisions, the full stack sprint
3. **The Prototype Trap** — Why the initial 15-category taxonomy failed
4. **Taxonomy Evolution** — Free-form → normalized → curated. The three-phase journey.
5. **The Attribution Problem** — Separating WHAT from WHO. Actor-neutral descriptions. The 3-step decision tree.
6. **Prompt Engineering for Classification** — Contrastive guidance, baseline exclusion, CoT-to-DB
7. **Prompt Quality Analysis** — 5 dimension scores, two-layer output (takeaways vs findings)
8. **Weekly Reflections** — ISO week navigation, making it a habit not a one-time report
9. **Closing the Loop** — Running Reflect on my own sessions, integrating the output
10. **What's Next** — Progress tracking, friction-to-pattern affinity maps

**Publish at:** code-insights.app/blog or Medium

---

## Post 2 — Variant B: Contrastive Classification (Journey Version) ⭐ PREFERRED

**Title idea:** It took 3 iterations to get LLM classification right. Here's the technique that worked.

---

I'm building a feature that analyzes AI coding sessions across 5 tools and classifies friction points into categories. Getting that classification consistent took three iterations.

**Attempt 1: Let the LLM decide.**

Free-form output. "Classify the friction in this session." Across 40 sessions, the same concept appeared as: "api-misunderstanding", "API-Misunderstanding", "api-confusion", "misunderstood-api", "api-knowledge-gap." Agent coordination issues split into: "agent-orchestration-failure", "agent-communication-failure", "agent-communication-breakdown", "agent-lifecycle-issue", "agent-shutdown-failure."

15 intended categories became 40+ variants. Cross-session aggregation was meaningless.

**Attempt 2: Define the categories + normalize the output.**

I curated 15 canonical categories and built a normalization layer — fuzzy string matching, alias maps that remap variants like "missing-dependency", "config-drift", "stale-cache", "version-mismatch" all to a single "stale-assumptions" category.

Better — but still inconsistent at the boundaries. "wrong-approach" and "knowledge-gap" got swapped constantly. Both involve the AI doing something incorrect. The descriptions overlapped. Same with "context-loss" vs "stale-assumptions" — both involve working from outdated information.

15 categories was also too many. Several were symptoms of the same root cause.

**Attempt 3: Fewer categories. Contrastive definitions. Attribution as a separate dimension.**

Consolidated to 9 categories: wrong-approach, knowledge-gap, stale-assumptions, incomplete-requirements, context-loss, scope-creep, repeated-mistakes, documentation-gap, tooling-limitation.

But the real shift was defining each category by what it ISN'T — relative to its most confusable neighbor:

*"If the AI had the right information but selected the wrong method → wrong-approach. If the AI lacked critical information that would have changed the approach entirely → knowledge-gap."*

*"If prior decisions from earlier in the session were forgotten → context-loss. If work started from assumptions about external state that turned out to be wrong → stale-assumptions."*

This is contrastive guidance — decision boundaries between confusable pairs, not descriptions of category centers. It mirrors how human experts handle ambiguous cases: by ruling out the closest alternative.

And instead of baking WHO caused the friction into the category name, we separated it into an independent attribution field: user-actionable, ai-capability, or environmental. Same "wrong-approach" category, but now you can filter by whether better user input would have prevented it or whether the AI failed despite clear instructions.

Classification consistency jumped. Not because we found better categories — because we changed the decision logic.

**These 9 categories are what we're shipping.** I'd genuinely like to hear: do they match the friction patterns you see in your AI coding sessions? What's missing? What would you rename?

wrong-approach · knowledge-gap · stale-assumptions · incomplete-requirements · context-loss · scope-creep · repeated-mistakes · documentation-gap · tooling-limitation

---

## Post 2 — Variant C: Condensed (All Three Principles)

**Title idea:** Three design principles from building LLM-powered session analysis

---

I'm building a feature that analyzes AI coding sessions across 5 tools — Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI, Copilot CLI, VS Code Copilot Chat — to surface friction patterns and effective techniques. Three design principles emerged that I think apply broadly.

**1. Separate computation from synthesis.** Aggregate in SQL. Synthesize with the LLM. Never ask the model to count, rank, or compute percentages — pass it pre-computed statistics and let it interpret. Same reason you don't embed business logic in the view layer: testability, debuggability, trust.

**2. Design taxonomies with contrastive definitions.** Describing what each category means creates overlap. Defining what each category ISN'T — relative to its most confusable neighbor — draws decision boundaries. "If the AI had the right info but chose wrong → wrong-approach. If it lacked critical info → knowledge-gap." Classification consistency improved significantly.

**3. Separate the WHAT from the WHO.** Every friction point has two orthogonal dimensions: what happened (category) and who contributed (attribution: user-actionable, ai-capability, or environmental). Baking the actor into the category doubles your taxonomy and makes progress tracking impossible — you can't isolate "am I getting better?" from "is the AI getting better?"

These compound. Deterministic aggregation feeds contrastive classification feeds orthogonal attribution. Each principle makes the next one work.

Building this in the open as part of Code Insights. **Would love to hear from others — what design patterns have you found essential when building LLM-powered analysis features?**

---

## Posting Notes

### Post 2 (This week — conversation starter)
- **Angle:** "Thinking out loud" about 3 hard problems. Invites engagement. Does NOT announce anything.
- **Visuals:** Optional — could do a simple before/after of messy vs clean categories, or skip visuals entirely (text-only posts often perform well for thought pieces)
- **CTA:** Open question — "How do you handle output consistency?" + "Has anyone tackled attribution?"
- **Engagement plan:** Reply to every comment within first 2 hours. Ask follow-up questions. Let the conversation run 1-2 days.

### Post 3 (2-3 days after Post 2)
- **Angle:** The payoff. What Reflect found. The closing-the-loop story. Teases v4 + blog.
- **Visuals:** Dashboard screenshot showing friction categories + attribution, or the Patterns page hero card
- **CTA:** Dual — "Full deep-dive coming with the release" + GitHub link for the curious

### Blog Post (Release day)
- **Angle:** Comprehensive technical narrative. The canonical reference.
- **Visuals:** Architecture diagram, before/after screenshots, taxonomy table
- **CTA:** Install command + "try it on your own sessions"

### Shared
- **Tone:** Authentic, educational, builder-first. NOT marketing. NOT "we're better than X."
- **Tags:** #ClaudeCode #DeveloperTools #OpenSource #AIEngineering #BuildInPublic #LLM

- New CLAUDE.md sections — Pre-Action Verification, Retry Discipline — directly addressing my top friction categories
- A hookify rule that fires a warning before any git checkout to an existing branch
- Updated agent prompts — my engineer agent no longer hardcodes main, my PM agent escalates unresponsive sub-agents instead of waiting

The insights went from "interesting report" to "enforced workflow rules" in the same session. That's the difference between reading about your patterns and actually fixing them.

**The LLM engineering lesson I keep coming back to:**

Save the LLM's chain-of-thought reasoning to your database. We added a `_reasoning` field that the model fills before classifying — it's never shown to users, but it creates a prompt-tuning dataset for free. When a friction point is miscategorized, I can see exactly what the model was thinking and adjust the prompt. Cost: ~190 extra tokens per call. Value: eliminates guesswork when debugging classification quality.

This feature is called "Reflect" and it's coming to Code Insights as part of v4.0. It works across all 5 supported AI tools — Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI, Copilot CLI, and VS Code Copilot Chat. Fully local, no cloud, no accounts.

Release and a full technical deep-dive blog post coming soon.

---

## Blog Post: The Full Journey (Ships with v4.0)

**Title:** Building Cross-Session AI Analysis: From Claude Code's /insights to Code Insights v4.0

**Format:** 1,500-2,500 word narrative technical post (Linear/Notion engineering blog style)

**Outline:**

1. **The Catalyst** — Running `/insights`, what it showed, the gap analysis
2. **The 4-Day Build** — Architecture decisions, the full stack sprint
3. **The Prototype Trap** — Why the initial 15-category taxonomy failed
4. **Taxonomy Evolution** — Free-form → normalized → curated. The three-phase journey.
5. **The Attribution Problem** — Separating WHAT from WHO. Actor-neutral descriptions. The 3-step decision tree.
6. **Prompt Engineering for Classification** — Contrastive guidance, baseline exclusion, CoT-to-DB
7. **Prompt Quality Analysis** — 5 dimension scores, two-layer output (takeaways vs findings)
8. **Weekly Reflections** — ISO week navigation, making it a habit not a one-time report
9. **Closing the Loop** — Running Reflect on my own sessions, integrating the output
10. **What's Next** — Progress tracking, friction-to-pattern affinity maps

**Publish at:** code-insights.app/blog or Medium

---

## Post 2 — Variant B: Contrastive Classification (Journey Version) ⭐ PREFERRED

**Title idea:** It took 3 iterations to get LLM classification right. Here's the technique that worked.

---

I'm building a feature that analyzes AI coding sessions across 5 tools and classifies friction points into categories. Getting that classification consistent took three iterations.

**Attempt 1: Let the LLM decide.**

Free-form output. "Classify the friction in this session." Across 40 sessions, the same concept appeared as: "api-misunderstanding", "API-Misunderstanding", "api-confusion", "misunderstood-api", "api-knowledge-gap." Agent coordination issues split into: "agent-orchestration-failure", "agent-communication-failure", "agent-communication-breakdown", "agent-lifecycle-issue", "agent-shutdown-failure."

15 intended categories became 40+ variants. Cross-session aggregation was meaningless.

**Attempt 2: Define the categories + normalize the output.**

I curated 15 canonical categories and built a normalization layer — fuzzy string matching, alias maps that remap variants like "missing-dependency", "config-drift", "stale-cache", "version-mismatch" all to a single "stale-assumptions" category.

Better — but still inconsistent at the boundaries. "wrong-approach" and "knowledge-gap" got swapped constantly. Both involve the AI doing something incorrect. The descriptions overlapped. Same with "context-loss" vs "stale-assumptions" — both involve working from outdated information.

15 categories was also too many. Several were symptoms of the same root cause.

**Attempt 3: Fewer categories. Contrastive definitions. Attribution as a separate dimension.**

Consolidated to 9 categories: wrong-approach, knowledge-gap, stale-assumptions, incomplete-requirements, context-loss, scope-creep, repeated-mistakes, documentation-gap, tooling-limitation.

But the real shift was defining each category by what it ISN'T — relative to its most confusable neighbor:

*"If the AI had the right information but selected the wrong method → wrong-approach. If the AI lacked critical information that would have changed the approach entirely → knowledge-gap."*

*"If prior decisions from earlier in the session were forgotten → context-loss. If work started from assumptions about external state that turned out to be wrong → stale-assumptions."*

This is contrastive guidance — decision boundaries between confusable pairs, not descriptions of category centers. It mirrors how human experts handle ambiguous cases: by ruling out the closest alternative.

And instead of baking WHO caused the friction into the category name, we separated it into an independent attribution field: user-actionable, ai-capability, or environmental. Same "wrong-approach" category, but now you can filter by whether better user input would have prevented it or whether the AI failed despite clear instructions.

Classification consistency jumped. Not because we found better categories — because we changed the decision logic.

**These 9 categories are what we're shipping.** I'd genuinely like to hear: do they match the friction patterns you see in your AI coding sessions? What's missing? What would you rename?

wrong-approach · knowledge-gap · stale-assumptions · incomplete-requirements · context-loss · scope-creep · repeated-mistakes · documentation-gap · tooling-limitation

---

## Post 2 — Variant C: Condensed (All Three Principles)

**Title idea:** Three design principles from building LLM-powered session analysis

---

I'm building a feature that analyzes AI coding sessions across 5 tools — Claude Code, Cursor, Codex CLI, Copilot CLI, VS Code Copilot Chat — to surface friction patterns and effective techniques. Three design principles emerged that I think apply broadly.

**1. Separate computation from synthesis.** Aggregate in SQL. Synthesize with the LLM. Never ask the model to count, rank, or compute percentages — pass it pre-computed statistics and let it interpret. Same reason you don't embed business logic in the view layer: testability, debuggability, trust.

**2. Design taxonomies with contrastive definitions.** Describing what each category means creates overlap. Defining what each category ISN'T — relative to its most confusable neighbor — draws decision boundaries. "If the AI had the right info but chose wrong → wrong-approach. If it lacked critical info → knowledge-gap." Classification consistency improved significantly.

**3. Separate the WHAT from the WHO.** Every friction point has two orthogonal dimensions: what happened (category) and who contributed (attribution: user-actionable, ai-capability, or environmental). Baking the actor into the category doubles your taxonomy and makes progress tracking impossible — you can't isolate "am I getting better?" from "is the AI getting better?"

These compound. Deterministic aggregation feeds contrastive classification feeds orthogonal attribution. Each principle makes the next one work.

Building this in the open as part of Code Insights. **Would love to hear from others — what design patterns have you found essential when building LLM-powered analysis features?**

---

## Posting Notes

### Post 2 (This week — conversation starter)
- **Angle:** "Thinking out loud" about 3 hard problems. Invites engagement. Does NOT announce anything.
- **Visuals:** Optional — could do a simple before/after of messy vs clean categories, or skip visuals entirely (text-only posts often perform well for thought pieces)
- **CTA:** Open question — "How do you handle output consistency?" + "Has anyone tackled attribution?"
- **Engagement plan:** Reply to every comment within first 2 hours. Ask follow-up questions. Let the conversation run 1-2 days.

### Post 3 (2-3 days after Post 2)
- **Angle:** The payoff. What Reflect found. The closing-the-loop story. Teases v4 + blog.
- **Visuals:** Dashboard screenshot showing friction categories + attribution, or the Patterns page hero card
- **CTA:** Dual — "Full deep-dive coming with the release" + GitHub link for the curious

### Blog Post (Release day)
- **Angle:** Comprehensive technical narrative. The canonical reference.
- **Visuals:** Architecture diagram, before/after screenshots, taxonomy table
- **CTA:** Install command + "try it on your own sessions"

### Shared
- **Tone:** Authentic, educational, builder-first. NOT marketing. NOT "we're better than X."
- **Tags:** #ClaudeCode #DeveloperTools #OpenSource #AIEngineering #BuildInPublic #LLM

content.text:
`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
Now I can see the full picture. Key observations for the plan:
1. **MarketingPage.tsx** is dead simple — just a list of section components in order. Adding ReflectSection is a one-line import + insertion.
2. **FeaturesSection** uses `AnimatedSection` with staggered delays — we replicate this for the three-beat cards.
3. **ProofSection** shows the "dashboard mockup" pattern — static mock data rendered as styled HTML/CSS cards. The ReflectSection demo card follows the same approach.
4. **MultiSourceSection** has `bg-gradient-to-b from-transparent via-accent/30 to-transparent` — this needs to flip to transparent since ReflectSection takes that background slot.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

---

## Implementation Plan: ReflectSection Landing Page Component

Based on the UX spec and the existing component patterns, here's the plan:

### Files to Create

| File | Description |
|------|-------------|
| `src/components/marketing/ReflectSection.tsx` | New section component (~200 lines) |

### Files to Modify

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/components/marketing/MarketingPage.tsx` | Import + insert `<ReflectSection />` between `FeaturesSection` and `MultiSourceSection` |
| `src/components/marketing/MultiSourceSection.tsx` | Remove `bg-gradient-to-b from-transparent via-accent/30 to-transparent` (flip to transparent to maintain alternating rhythm) |

### ReflectSection.tsx Structure

```
ReflectSection
├── Section wrapper (py-14 lg:py-20, via-accent/30 gradient background)
├── Header (AnimatedSection)
│   ├── Badge: "New in v4.0" (pill, primary accent)
│   ├── Heading: "Your sessions have patterns. Reflect finds them."
│   └── Subheading
├── Three-Beat Cards (grid md:grid-cols-3, AnimatedSection staggered)
│   ├── Analyze card (Search icon + friction category mini-list)
│   ├── Synthesize card (TrendingUp icon + week snapshot mini-card)
│   └── Generate card (FileCode2 icon + rules code block)
└── Visual Demo Card (AnimatedSection, hidden on mobile)
    ├── Dashboard frame header ("Weekly Reflect: 2026-W10")
    ├── Working style tagline (italic quote)
    ├── Two-column: Top Patterns (emerald bars) | Top Friction (amber bars)
    ├── Generated Rules preview (pre/monospace code block)
    └── Bottom gradient fade
└── Mobile Quote Card (shown only on < md)
    ├── Working style tagline
    └── "Learn more in the docs" link
```

### Design Decisions (From UX Spec)

- **Card styling**: Reuses exact FeaturesSection card classes (`rounded-xl border border-border/50 bg-card/50 backdrop-blur-sm...`)
- **Demo bars**: Simple `div` width percentages, NOT Recharts (too heavy for static demo)
- **Rules preview**: `<pre>` with monospace font, NOT react-syntax-highlighter
- **All data is hardcoded** — no API calls, no React Query
- **Demo card is `aria-hidden="true"`** (decorative)
- **Animation**: Same `AnimatedSection` wrapper with staggered delays (0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.2)

### What's NOT Changing

- Hero section — untouched
- ProofSection — already updated in PR #125
- FeaturesSection — already updated in PR #125
- No new dependencies needed (Lucide icons already available)

---

3 files total (1 new, 2 modified). Straightforward component work following established patterns. Should I kick off `/start-feature` or is this small enough to implement directly?

input.prompt:
Thoroughness: very thorough

I need to compare the ACTUAL insight output structures from the Code Insights server/CLI against what the landing page marketing components show as mock data.

## What to find

1. **Decision insight structure** — find the actual schema/type for Decision insights. Look in:
   - `server/src/llm/` for prompt definitions that define what a "decision" insight contains (fields like situation, choice, alternatives, tradeoffs, revisit)
   - `cli/src/types/` or `server/src/types/` for InsightMetadata types
   - `server/src/llm/response-parsers.ts` or similar for how decisions are parsed
   - `dashboard/src/` for how decisions are rendered in the actual dashboard

2. **Learning insight structure** — same as above but for Learning insights (fields like symptom, rootCause, takeaway, appliesWhen)

3. **Prompt Quality structure** — the NEW taxonomy (NOT the old single-score model). Find:
   - The 5 dimension scores and their exact field names
   - The deficit/strength category names
   - The takeaway structure (before/after)
   - Look in `server/src/llm/prompt-constants.ts`, `server/src/llm/prompt-quality-normalize.ts`
   - Dashboard rendering in `dashboard/src/` (PromptQualityCard or similar)

4. **Session facets structure** — friction points and effective patterns:
   - FrictionPoint type (category, description, attribution fields)
   - EffectivePattern type (category, description, driver fields)
   - Look in `cli/src/types/` and `server/src/llm/prompt-constants.ts`

5. **Reflect synthesis output** — what does a weekly reflect snapshot contain:
   - Rules, skills, working style tagline
   - Look in `server/src/routes/` for reflect API response shapes

For each structure found, note the EXACT field names and types. I need to compare these against the marketing mock data in the landing page.

1→// Canonical category arrays and classification guidance strings for LLM analysis.
     2→// Extracted from prompts.ts — imported by normalizers and prompt generators.
     3→
     4→// Shared guidance for friction category and attribution classification.
     5→// Actor-neutral category definitions describe the gap, not the actor.
     6→// Attribution field captures who contributed to the friction for actionability.
     7→export const FRICTION_CLASSIFICATION_GUIDANCE = `
     8→FRICTION CLASSIFICATION GUIDANCE:
     9→
    10→Each friction point captures WHAT went wrong (category + description), WHO contributed (attribution), and WHY you classified it that way (_reasoning).
    11→
    12→CATEGORIES — classify the TYPE of gap or obstacle:
    13→- "wrong-approach": A strategy was pursued that didn't fit the task — wrong architecture, wrong tool, wrong pattern
    14→- "knowledge-gap": Incorrect knowledge was applied about a library, API, framework, or language feature
    15→- "stale-assumptions": Work proceeded from assumptions about current state that were incorrect (stale files, changed config, different environment)
    16→- "incomplete-requirements": Instructions were missing critical context, constraints, or acceptance criteria
    17→- "context-loss": Prior decisions or constraints established earlier in the session were lost or forgotten
    18→- "scope-creep": Work expanded beyond the boundaries of the stated task
    19→- "repeated-mistakes": The same or similar error occurred multiple times despite earlier correction
    20→- "documentation-gap": Relevant docs existed but were inaccessible or unfindable during the session
    21→- "tooling-limitation": The tool genuinely lacked a needed capability
    22→
    23→When no category fits, create a specific kebab-case category. A precise novel category is better than a vague canonical one.
    24→
    25→ATTRIBUTION — 3-step decision tree (follow IN ORDER):
    26→Step 1: Is the cause external to the user-AI interaction? (missing docs, broken tooling, infra outage) → "environmental"
    27→Step 2: Could the USER have prevented this with better input? Evidence: vague prompt, missing context, no constraints, late requirements, ambiguous correction → "user-actionable"
    28→Step 3: User input was clear and the AI still failed → "ai-capability"
    29→When genuinely mixed between user-actionable and ai-capability, lean "user-actionable" — this tool helps users improve.
    30→
    31→DESCRIPTION RULES:
    32→- One neutral sentence describing the GAP, not the actor
    33→- Include specific details (file names, APIs, error messages)
    34→- Frame as "Missing X caused Y" NOT "The AI failed to X" or "The user forgot to X"
    35→- Let the attribution field carry the who`;
    36→
    37→export const CANONICAL_FRICTION_CATEGORIES = [
    38→  'wrong-approach',
    39→  'knowledge-gap',
    40→  'stale-assumptions',
    41→  'incomplete-requirements',
    42→  'context-loss',
    43→  'scope-creep',
    44→  'repeated-mistakes',
    45→  'documentation-gap',
    46→  'tooling-limitation',
    47→] as const;
    48→
    49→export const CANONICAL_PATTERN_CATEGORIES = [
    50→  'structured-planning',
    51→  'incremental-implementation',
    52→  'verification-workflow',
    53→  'systematic-debugging',
    54→  'self-correction',
    55→  'context-gathering',
    56→  'domain-expertise',
    57→  'effective-tooling',
    58→] as const;
    59→
    60→export const CANONICAL_PQ_DEFICIT_CATEGORIES = [
    61→  'vague-request',
    62→  'missing-context',
    63→  'late-constraint',
    64→  'unclear-correction',
    65→  'scope-drift',
    66→  'missing-acceptance-criteria',
    67→  'assumption-not-surfaced',
    68→] as const;
    69→
    70→export const CANONICAL_PQ_STRENGTH_CATEGORIES = [
    71→  'precise-request',
    72→  'effective-context',
    73→  'productive-correction',
    74→] as const;
    75→
    76→export const CANONICAL_PQ_CATEGORIES = [
    77→  ...CANONICAL_PQ_DEFICIT_CATEGORIES,
    78→  ...CANONICAL_PQ_STRENGTH_CATEGORIES,
    79→] as const;
    80→
    81→export const PROMPT_QUALITY_CLASSIFICATION_GUIDANCE = `
    82→PROMPT QUALITY CLASSIFICATION GUIDANCE:
    83→
    84→Each finding captures a specific moment where the user's prompting either caused friction (deficit) or enabled productivity (strength).
    85→
    86→DEFICIT CATEGORIES — classify prompting problems:
    87→- "vague-request": Request lacked specificity needed for the AI to act without guessing. Missing file paths, function names, expected behavior, or concrete details.
    88→  NOT this category if the AI had enough context to succeed but failed anyway — that is an AI capability issue, not a prompting issue.
    89→
    90→- "missing-context": Critical background knowledge about architecture, conventions, dependencies, or current state was not provided.
    91→  NOT this category if the information was available in the codebase and the AI could have found it by reading files — that is an AI context-gathering failure.
    92→
    93→- "late-constraint": A requirement or constraint was provided AFTER the AI had already started implementing a different approach, causing rework.
    94→  NOT this category if the constraint was genuinely discovered during implementation (requirements changed). Only classify if the user KNEW the constraint before the session started.
    95→
    96→- "unclear-correction": The user told the AI its output was wrong without explaining what was wrong or why. "That's not right", "try again", "no" without context.
    97→  NOT this category if the user gave a brief but sufficient correction ("use map instead of forEach" is clear enough).
    98→
    99→- "scope-drift": The session objective shifted mid-conversation, or multiple unrelated objectives were addressed in one session.
   100→  NOT this category if the user is working through logically connected subtasks of one objective.
   101→
   102→- "missing-acceptance-criteria": The user did not define what successful completion looks like, leading to back-and-forth about whether the output meets expectations.
   103→  NOT this category for exploratory sessions where the user is discovering what they want.
   104→
   105→- "assumption-not-surfaced": The user held an unstated assumption that the AI could not reasonably infer from code or conversation.
   106→  NOT this category if the assumption was reasonable for the AI to make (e.g., standard coding conventions).
   107→
   108→STRENGTH CATEGORIES — classify prompting successes (only when notably above average):
   109→- "precise-request": Request included enough specificity (file paths, function names, expected behavior, error messages) that the AI could act correctly on the first attempt.
   110→
   111→- "effective-context": User proactively shared architecture, conventions, prior decisions, or current state that the AI demonstrably used to make better decisions.
   112→
   113→- "productive-correction": When the AI went off track, the user provided a correction that included WHAT was wrong, WHY, and enough context for the AI to redirect effectively on the next response.
   114→
   115→CONTRASTIVE PAIRS:
   116→- vague-request vs missing-context: Was the problem in HOW THE TASK WAS DESCRIBED (vague-request) or WHAT BACKGROUND KNOWLEDGE WAS ABSENT (missing-context)?
   117→- late-constraint vs missing-context: Did the user EVENTUALLY provide it in the same session? Yes → late-constraint. Never → missing-context.
   118→- missing-context vs assumption-not-surfaced: Is this a FACT the user could have copy-pasted (missing-context), or a BELIEF/PREFERENCE they held (assumption-not-surfaced)?
   119→- scope-drift vs missing-acceptance-criteria: Did the user try to do TOO MANY THINGS (scope-drift) or ONE THING WITHOUT DEFINING SUCCESS (missing-acceptance-criteria)?
   120→- unclear-correction vs vague-request: Was this the user's FIRST MESSAGE about this task (vague-request) or a RESPONSE TO AI OUTPUT (unclear-correction)?
   121→
   122→DIMENSION SCORING (0-100):
   123→- context_provision: How well did the user provide relevant background upfront?
   124→  90+: Proactively shared architecture, constraints, conventions. 50-69: Notable gaps causing detours. <30: No context, AI working blind.
   125→- request_specificity: How precise were task requests?
   126→  90+: File paths, expected behavior, scope boundaries. 50-69: Mix of specific and vague. <30: Nearly all requests lacked detail.
   127→- scope_management: How focused was the session?
   128→  90+: Single clear objective, logical progression. 50-69: Some drift but primary goal met. <30: Unfocused, no clear objective.
   129→- information_timing: Were requirements provided when needed?
   130→  90+: All constraints front-loaded before implementation. 50-69: Some important requirements late. <30: Requirements drip-fed, constant corrections.
   131→- correction_quality: How well did the user redirect the AI?
   132→  90+: Corrections included what, why, and context. 50-69: Mix of clear and unclear. <30: Corrections gave almost no signal.
   133→  Score 75 if no corrections were needed (absence of corrections in a successful session = good prompting).
   134→
   135→EDGE CASES:
   136→- Short sessions (<5 user messages): Score conservatively. Do not penalize for missing elements unnecessary in quick tasks.
   137→- Exploration sessions: Do not penalize for missing acceptance criteria or scope drift.
   138→- Sessions where AI performed well despite vague prompts: Still classify deficits. Impact should be "low" since no visible cost.
   139→- Agentic/delegation sessions: If the user gave a clear high-level directive and the AI autonomously planned and executed successfully, do not penalize for low message count or lack of micro-level specificity. Effective delegation IS good prompting. Focus on the quality of the initial delegation prompt.`;
   140→
   141→export const EFFECTIVE_PATTERN_CLASSIFICATION_GUIDANCE = `
   142→EFFECTIVE PATTERN CLASSIFICATION GUIDANCE:
   143→
   144→Each effective pattern captures a technique or approach that contributed to a productive session outcome.
   145→
   146→BASELINE EXCLUSION — do NOT classify these as patterns:
   147→- Routine file reads at session start (Read/Glob/Grep on <5 files before editing)
   148→- Following explicit user instructions (user said "run tests" → running tests is not a pattern)
   149→- Basic tool usage (single file edits, standard CLI commands)
   150→- Trivial self-corrections (typo fixes, minor syntax errors caught immediately)
   151→Only classify behavior that is NOTABLY thorough, strategic, or beyond baseline expectations.
   152→
   153→CATEGORIES — classify the TYPE of effective pattern:
   154→- "structured-planning": Decomposed the task into explicit steps, defined scope boundaries, or established a plan BEFORE writing code. Signal: plan/task-list/scope-definition appears before implementation.
   155→- "incremental-implementation": Work progressed in small, verifiable steps with validation between them. Signal: multiple small edits with checks between, not one large batch.
   156→- "verification-workflow": Proactive correctness checks (builds, tests, linters, types) BEFORE considering work complete. Signal: test/build/lint commands when nothing was known broken.
   157→- "systematic-debugging": Methodical investigation using structured techniques (binary search, log insertion, reproduction isolation). Signal: multiple targeted diagnostic steps, not random guessing.
   158→- "self-correction": Recognized a wrong path and pivoted WITHOUT user correction. Signal: explicit acknowledgment of mistake + approach change. NOT this if the user pointed out the error.
   159→- "context-gathering": NOTABLY thorough investigation before changes — reading 5+ files, cross-module exploration, schema/type/config review. Signal: substantial Read/Grep/Glob usage spanning multiple directories before any Edit/Write.
   160→- "domain-expertise": Applied specific framework/API/language knowledge correctly on first attempt without searching. Signal: correct non-obvious API usage with no preceding search and no subsequent error. NOT this if files were read first — that is context-gathering.
   161→- "effective-tooling": Leveraged advanced tool capabilities that multiplied productivity — agent delegation, parallel work, multi-file coordination, strategic mode selection. Signal: use of tool features beyond basic read/write/edit.
   162→
   163→CONTRASTIVE PAIRS:
   164→- structured-planning vs incremental-implementation: Planning = DECIDING what to do (before). Incremental = HOW you execute (during). Can have one without the other.
   165→- context-gathering vs domain-expertise: Gathering = ACTIVE INVESTIGATION (reading files). Expertise = APPLYING EXISTING KNOWLEDGE without investigation. If files were read first → context-gathering.
   166→- verification-workflow vs systematic-debugging: Verification = PROACTIVE (checking working code). Debugging = REACTIVE (investigating a failure).
   167→- self-correction vs user-directed: Self-correction = AI caught own mistake unprompted. User said "that's wrong" → NOT self-correction.
   168→
   169→DRIVER — 4-step decision tree (follow IN ORDER):
   170→Step 1: Did user infrastructure enable this? (CLAUDE.md rules, agent configs, hookify hooks, custom commands, system prompts) → "user-driven"
   171→Step 2: Did the user explicitly request this behavior? (asked for plan, requested tests, directed investigation) → "user-driven"
   172→Step 3: Did the AI exhibit this without any user prompting or infrastructure? → "ai-driven"
   173→Step 4: Both made distinct, identifiable contributions → "collaborative"
   174→Use "collaborative" ONLY when you can name what EACH party contributed. If uncertain, prefer the more specific label.
   175→
   176→When no canonical category fits, create a specific kebab-case category (a precise novel category is better than forcing a poor fit).`;
   177→

1→// Prompt template strings and generator functions for LLM session analysis.
     2→// Types → prompt-types.ts, constants → prompt-constants.ts,
     3→// formatting → message-format.ts, parsers → response-parsers.ts.
     4→
     5→import type { SessionMetadata } from './prompt-types.js';
     6→import type { ContentBlock } from './types.js';
     7→import {
     8→  FRICTION_CLASSIFICATION_GUIDANCE,
     9→  CANONICAL_FRICTION_CATEGORIES,
    10→  CANONICAL_PATTERN_CATEGORIES,
    11→  CANONICAL_PQ_DEFICIT_CATEGORIES,
    12→  CANONICAL_PQ_STRENGTH_CATEGORIES,
    13→  PROMPT_QUALITY_CLASSIFICATION_GUIDANCE,
    14→  EFFECTIVE_PATTERN_CLASSIFICATION_GUIDANCE,
    15→} from './prompt-constants.js';
    16→import { formatSessionMetaLine } from './message-format.js';
    17→
    18→// =============================================================================
    19→// SHARED SYSTEM PROMPT
    20→// A minimal (~100 token) system prompt shared by all analysis calls.
    21→// The full classification guidance and schema examples live in the instruction
    22→// suffix (user[1]), keeping the system prompt cacheable across calls.
    23→// =============================================================================
    24→
    25→/**
    26→ * Shared system prompt for all LLM analysis calls.
    27→ * Paired with buildCacheableConversationBlock() + an analysis-specific instruction block.
    28→ */
    29→export const SHARED_ANALYST_SYSTEM_PROMPT = `You are a senior staff engineer analyzing an AI coding session. You will receive the conversation transcript followed by specific extraction instructions. Respond with valid JSON only, wrapped in <json>...</json> tags.`;
    30→
    31→// =============================================================================
    32→// CACHEABLE CONVERSATION BLOCK
    33→// Wraps the formatted conversation in an Anthropic ephemeral cache block.
    34→// CRITICAL: Must contain ONLY the formatted messages — no project name, no session
    35→// metadata, no per-session variables. This ensures cache hits across sessions.
    36→// =============================================================================
    37→
    38→/**
    39→ * Wrap formatted conversation messages in a cacheable content block.
    40→ * The cache_control field instructs Anthropic to cache everything up to
    41→ * and including this block (ephemeral, 5-minute TTL).
    42→ *
    43→ * Non-Anthropic providers receive this as a ContentBlock[] and use
    44→ * flattenContent() to convert it to a plain string.
    45→ *
    46→ * @param formattedMessages - Output of formatMessagesForAnalysis()
    47→ */
    48→export function buildCacheableConversationBlock(formattedMessages: string): ContentBlock {
    49→  return {
    50→    type: 'text',
    51→    // Trailing double newline ensures the instruction block (user[1]) reads as a
    52→    // distinct section when providers flatten content blocks to a single string.
    53→    text: `--- CONVERSATION ---\n${formattedMessages}\n--- END CONVERSATION ---\n\n`,
    54→    cache_control: { type: 'ephemeral' },
    55→  };
    56→}
    57→
    58→// =============================================================================
    59→// SESSION ANALYSIS INSTRUCTIONS
    60→// The instruction suffix for session analysis calls (user[1]).
    61→// Contains the full analyst persona, schema, and quality guidance.
    62→// Per-session variables (project name, summary, meta) go here — NOT in the
    63→// cached conversation block.
    64→// =============================================================================
    65→
    66→/**
    67→ * Build the instruction suffix for session analysis.
    68→ * Used as the second content block in the user message, after the cached conversation.
    69→ */
    70→export function buildSessionAnalysisInstructions(
    71→  projectName: string,
    72→  sessionSummary: string | null,
    73→  meta?: SessionMetadata
    74→): string {
    75→  return `You are a senior staff engineer writing entries for a team's engineering knowledge base. You've just observed an AI-assisted coding session and your job is to extract the insights that would save another engineer time if they encountered a similar situation 6 months from now.
    76→
    77→Your audience is a developer who has never seen this session but works on the same codebase. They need enough context to understand WHY a decision was made, WHAT specific gotcha was discovered, and WHEN this knowledge applies.
    78→
    79→Project: ${projectName}
    80→${sessionSummary ? `Session Summary: ${sessionSummary}\n` : ''}${formatSessionMetaLine(meta)}
    81→=== PART 1: SESSION FACETS ===
    82→Extract these FIRST as a holistic session assessment:
    83→
    84→1. outcome_satisfaction: Rate the session outcome.
    85→   - "high": Task completed successfully, user satisfied
    86→   - "medium": Partial completion or minor issues
    87→   - "low": Significant problems, user frustrated
    88→   - "abandoned": Session ended without achieving the goal
    89→
    90→2. workflow_pattern: Identify the dominant workflow pattern (or null if unclear).
    91→   Recommended values: "plan-then-implement", "iterative-refinement", "debug-fix-verify", "explore-then-build", "direct-execution"
    92→
    93→3. friction_points: Identify up to 5 moments where progress was blocked or slowed (array, max 5).
    94→   Each friction point has:
    95→   - _reasoning: (REQUIRED) Your reasoning chain for category + attribution. 2-3 sentences max. Walk through the decision tree steps. This field is saved but not shown to users — use it to think before classifying.
    96→   - category: Use one of these PREFERRED categories when applicable: ${CANONICAL_FRICTION_CATEGORIES.join(', ')}. Create a new kebab-case category only when none of these fit.
    97→   - attribution: "user-actionable" (better user input would have prevented this), "ai-capability" (AI failed despite adequate input), or "environmental" (external constraint)
    98→   - description: One neutral sentence describing what happened, with specific details (file names, APIs, errors)
    99→   - severity: "high" (blocked progress for multiple turns), "medium" (caused a detour), "low" (minor hiccup)
   100→   - resolution: "resolved" (fixed in session), "workaround" (bypassed), "unresolved" (still broken)
   101→${FRICTION_CLASSIFICATION_GUIDANCE}
   102→
   103→4. effective_patterns: Up to 3 techniques or approaches that worked particularly well (array, max 3).
   104→   Each has:
   105→   - _reasoning: (REQUIRED) Your reasoning chain for category + driver. 2-3 sentences max. Walk through the decision tree steps and baseline exclusion check. This field is saved but not shown to users — use it to think before classifying.
   106→   - category: Use one of these PREFERRED categories when applicable: structured-planning, incremental-implementation, verification-workflow, systematic-debugging, self-correction, context-gathering, domain-expertise, effective-tooling. Create a new kebab-case category only when none fit.
   107→   - description: Specific technique worth repeating (1-2 sentences with concrete detail)
   108→   - confidence: 0-100 how confident you are this is genuinely effective
   109→   - driver: Who drove this pattern — "user-driven" (user explicitly requested it), "ai-driven" (AI exhibited it without prompting), or "collaborative" (both contributed or emerged from interaction)
   110→${EFFECTIVE_PATTERN_CLASSIFICATION_GUIDANCE}
   111→
   112→5. had_course_correction: true if the user redirected the AI from a wrong approach, false otherwise
   113→6. course_correction_reason: If had_course_correction is true, briefly explain what was corrected (or null)
   114→7. iteration_count: Number of times the user had to clarify, correct, or re-explain something
   115→
   116→If the session has minimal friction and straightforward execution, use empty arrays for friction_points, set outcome_satisfaction to "high", and iteration_count to 0.
   117→
   118→=== PART 2: INSIGHTS ===
   119→Then extract these:
   120→
   121→You will extract:
   122→1. **Summary**: A narrative of what was accomplished and the outcome
   123→2. **Decisions**: Technical choices made — with full situation context, reasoning, rejected alternatives, trade-offs, and conditions for revisiting (max 3)
   124→3. **Learnings**: Technical discoveries, gotchas, debugging breakthroughs — with the observable symptom, root cause, and a transferable takeaway (max 5)
   125→
   126→Quality Standards:
   127→- Only include insights you would write in a team knowledge base for future reference
   128→- Each insight MUST reference concrete details: specific file names, library names, error messages, API endpoints, or code patterns
   129→- Do not invent file names, APIs, errors, or details not present in the conversation
   130→- Rate your confidence in each insight's value (0-100). Only include insights you rate 70+.
   131→- It is better to return 0 insights in a category than to include generic or trivial ones
   132→- If a session is straightforward with no notable decisions or learnings, say so in the summary and leave other categories empty
   133→
   134→Length Guidance:
   135→- Fill every field in the schema. An empty "trade_offs" or "revisit_when" is worse than a longer response.
   136→- Total response: stay under 2000 tokens. If you must cut, drop lower-confidence insights rather than compressing high-confidence ones.
   137→- Evidence: 1-3 short quotes per insight, referencing turn labels.
   138→- Prefer precision over brevity — a specific 3-sentence insight beats a vague 1-sentence insight.
   139→
   140→DO NOT include insights like these (too generic/trivial):
   141→- "Used debugging techniques to fix an issue"
   142→- "Made architectural decisions about the codebase"
   143→- "Implemented a new feature" (the summary already covers this)
   144→- "Used React hooks for state management" (too generic without specifics)
   145→- "Fixed a bug in the code" (what bug? what was the root cause?)
   146→- Anything that restates the task without adding transferable knowledge
   147→

Step 3: AI explored autonomously → ai-driven. Category: context-gathering (active investigation, not pre-existing knowledge).",

200→    "outcome": "success | partial | abandoned | blocked",

   237→// =============================================================================
   238→// PROMPT QUALITY INSTRUCTIONS
   239→// The instruction suffix for prompt quality analysis calls (user[1]).
